readings.source: CME Urea Granular FOB Egypt front-month ≈ $475.50/t (7–8 Jul), rebounding from mid-Jun lows near $420/t; renewed tanker safety concerns near Oman restoring risk premium. Subscription-grade assessments (Argus/ICIS) required for confirmed physical level — CME futures indicative.

June Yara–Mosaic contract confirmed at $775/t CFR, down $50/t from May's $825. July contract not yet publicly confirmed as of 8 Jul; trade press signals (Profercy, QCIntel) suggest possible +$25/t jump on North African supply squeeze — cannot independently verify. Monthly benchmark — paywalled for exact figure.
CME DAP FOB NOLA front-month ≈ $757.50/st (7–8 Jul), stabilising after falling from $782.50 (25 Jun); Aug $740, Sep $737.50 on the curve. Indicative — not a confirmed physical assessment.
No fresh public citations as of 8 Jul — holding prior indications (~$405–410/t CFR; secondary series $412.50, 25 Jun). Not exchange-traded; subscription-only for confirmed level.

readings.wow: w/w broadly stable (easing from 6 Jul peak)
readings.source: Front-month ≈ €44.0/MWh (8 Jul), easing after three-week high of €45.4 on 6 Jul; €42.57 on 29 Jun → w/w still up ~3.4%. European heat wave and below-average storage (~49% vs ~60% a year ago) underpin price, partially offset by easing geopolitical tensions along shipping routes.

editorialReading: The pressure across the hemisphere is easing — but showing a second pulse. Global urea (Egypt FOB ~$470–480/t) fell sharply from its May spike but is now rebounding as renewed tanker safety concerns near Oman revive a risk premium, a reminder that the war premium is fragile rather than fading cleanly. June Tampa ammonia settled at $775/t (down $50 on May), with trade signals pointing to a possible July jump on North African supply tightening. DAP has steadied near $757.50/st — NOLA-benchmarked, so directly relevant to US buyers — stabilising after a brief dip to $750. The US retains its structural edge (CF Industries' Donaldsonville and Yazoo City capacity, the Tampa benchmark), but the swing risk sits with import-dependent neighbours: Brazil is buying potash around $405–415/t CFR ahead of the Safrinha, and Mexico leans on Gulf nitrogen flows. The one line still elevated is TTF gas (~€44/MWh, easing from this week's €45.4 peak) — a European cost driver, but a reminder that the nitrogen complex's cost floor is energy, and any supply disruption that re-fires the war premium compounds that floor for the hemisphere's net importers.
watchNext: Whether urea extends its rebound toward ~$500/t or fades as ceasefire conditions stabilise, and whether a July Tampa ammonia contract confirms above June's $775/t — the Western-Hemisphere benchmark. Watch the CME DAP / urea curve for the US buy-side signal, Brazilian potash CFR into the Safrinha, and TTF gas as the global nitrogen cost floor. The squeeze on hemisphere food security runs less through US farmer prices than through import-dependent neighbours' access to affordable nitrogen. Sourcing note: real-time Argus / Green Markets assessments are subscription-only and the free World Bank Pink Sheet lags ~a month — these are publicly-cited indications, and the urea figure in particular is indicative, not a confirmed 8 July assessment.
methodologyNote: Weekly editorial refresh from World Bank Pink Sheet (monthly, free), Argus public citations, Reuters / Bloomberg quotes, IFA quarterly summaries, and USDA fertilizer outlook. Gold-standard real-time prices (CRU, Argus, ICIS, Profercy) are paywalled — ranges are aggregated from publicly-cited figures, not republished feeds. Editorial reading is our market interpretation.
